source_metadata.title_raw: Reuben E. Cruse, Jr. - A-19-179 - January 7, 2020 - Redding

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 7, 2020

Rubin E. Cruse, Jr.
County Counsel
County of Shasta
1450 Court Street, Suite 332
Redding, CA 96001-1675

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-179

Dear Mr. Cruse:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of County of Shasta Supervisor
Mary Rickert regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1
and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act's conflict of interest
provisions and Section 1090 and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also, note that we are
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts
relating to it to the Attorney General's Office and the Shasta County District Attorney's Office,
which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either
entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other
than the requestor." (Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

(1) Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Supervisor Rickert from taking part
in governmental decisions relating to a proposed residential development project, which would
divide the approximately 715-acre project site into 166 residential parcels and six large open space
parcels, given that the Supervisor has various financial interests associated with a 5,000-acre real
property located approximately one mile from the project site?

(2) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Supervisor from participating in the making of, or the
Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the project?

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

(1) No. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Supervisor from taking
part in decisions relating to the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions
would have a material financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests at issue based
on the facts presented.

(2) No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in the
making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the
project.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the Shasta County Counsel and the authorized representative of Shasta County
Supervisor Mary Rickert. A private developer has applied to the County for approval of a proposed
residential development project (the "Project") to be constructed on approximately 715 acres in an
unincorporated area of the County (the "Project Site"). Upon completion, the Project would divide
the Project Site into 166 residential parcels, ranging from 1.38 acres to 6.81 acres in size, and six
open space parcels totaling approximately 190 acres. An average residence constructed as part of
the Project would be approximately 3,550 square feet with 3.5 bedrooms.

The Shasta County Board of Supervisors will soon consider whether to approve the Project.
The developer must obtain the all of the following in order to proceed with the Project:

• Certification of an Environmental Impact Report for the Project.

• A zone amendment to change the Project Site's current zoning from Rural Residential Five-
Acre Minimum, Rural Residential Three-Acre Minimum, and Unclassified to a Planned
Development Zone District establishing a Conceptual Development Plan for the entire Project
Site.

• A Subdivision Map to divide the Project Site into the parcels described above.

• A development agreement between the County and developer for the funding of intersection
improvements. The proposed development agreement would require the developer to pay a pro-
rata share of the cost of signalization of an intersection in order to mitigate the Project's traffic
and circulation impacts. In consideration, the County would extend the effective period of the
Project's Subdivision Map for an additional 60 months for a total of 84 months.

The Supervisor's husband has an estimated 1/12 ownership interest in Rickert Ranch, LLC
("Rickert Ranch"), and that business owns the "Ranch Property," an approximately 5,000-acre real
property located approximately one mile from the Project Site. Rickert Ranch has an agreement
with a hunting club allowing the club to use the Ranch Property for hunting, and the Supervisor
estimates that Rickert Ranch receives $10,000 per year for permitting hunting activities on the
Ranch Property. The Ranch Property is subject to a conservation easement, which protects the
Ranch Property's natural resources and agricultural values by preventing its development.

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 3

Rickert Ranch leases the Ranch Property to Rickert Agricultural Services, Inc. ("RASI"),
which is owned by the Supervisor and her husband and provides professional farm management,
appraisal, and farm and ranch brokerage services. RASI's annual gross revenues vary from year to
year between $300,000 to $500,000 per year, and it pays $60,000 per year to Rickert Ranch to lease
the Ranch Property. RASI subleases the Ranch Property to Ralphs Ranches, doing business as
"Prather Ranch" (hereafter "Prather Ranch"), for cattle grazing. The supervisor and her husband are
the majority owners of Prather Ranch.

The Supervisor states that the Project would not have any impact on, and would not interfere
with, any of the current uses of the Ranch Property, and that the Project would not have any impact
on her, her husband, or her dependent children's personal finances. Prior to being elected, the
Supervisor made statements in support of preserving the County's rural lifestyle and in opposition
to the Project due to its impacts on that lifestyle. You provided additional information on October
18, 2019 and November 8, 2019.

ANALYSIS

The Act

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's financial
interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are identified in Section 87103. Of
those interests, the Supervisor has the following interests in decisions relating to the Project based
on the facts presented:

• An interest in Rickert Ranch as a business entity assuming her husband's ownership interest in
that business is worth $2,000 or more.² (See Section 87103(a).)

• An interest in RASI as a business entity. (See Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• An interest in Prather Ranch as a business entity assuming the Supervisor and her husband's
ownership interest in that business is worth $2,000 or more. (See Section 87103(a) and (d).)

• A source of income interest in RASI or Prather Ranch if the Supervisor receives $500 or more
in income from either of those businesses in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. (See
Section 87103(c).) In addition, because the Supervisor and her husband own more than 10
percent of RASI and Prather Ranch, the Supervisor also has a source of income in a customer
or client of the business if her pro rata share of the income received from the customer or client,

______________________________

² Section 82034 defines "investment" for purposes of the Act, and provides that "investment" means, among
other things, any investment in a business entity owned directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the official or his or her
immediate family if the business entity has a real property interest in the jurisdiction or does, has done in the previous
two years, or will do business in the jurisdiction. Section 82029 provides that "immediate family" includes the spouse
and dependent children.

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 4

including her community property interest in her husband's pro rata share, is $500 or more.³
(Section 82030(a).)

• A source of income interest in Rickert Ranch if the Supervisor receives $500 or more in the 12
months prior to the decision at issue including her community property interest in any income
received by her husband. (See Section 87103(c).)

• A real property interest in RASI's leasehold of the Ranch Property and a real property interest
in Prather Ranch's sub-leasehold of the Ranch Property.⁴ (See Sections 82033 and 87103(b).)

• An interest in the Supervisor's personal finances or those of immediate family. (See Section
87103.)

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest. In addition, an official's business interest is explicitly involved in any
decision affecting the business as described in Regulation 18702.1(a)(1), and an official's real
property interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting that real property as described in
Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the
decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

None of the Supervisor's financial interests at issue are explicitly involved in decisions
relating to the Project under Regulation 18701(b) or Regulation 18702.1(a)(1). Therefore, those
decisions effect on each of the Supervisor's interests at issue is reasonably foreseeable if it can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

³ You have provided no indication that a customer or client of the businesses will be implicated by the
decision. Accordingly, we cannot analyze potential conflicts resulting from a customer or client of the business. If a
decision may implicate a customer or client of the business, the Supervisor should seek further advice.

⁴ Section 82033 provides that an individual's interest in real property include "a prorate share of interests in
real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." Section 82033 further provides that an "interest in real property" includes
"any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the
jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family
if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." As you have indicated that Rickert
Ranch owns the Ranch Property and the Supervisor's husband has only a 1/12 ownership interest in Rickert Ranch, the
supervisor does not appear to have an interest in the property arising out of her husband's interest in the business.
Nonetheless, the Supervisor does have property interests in the Ranch Property as tenants under the respective leases.

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 5

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's inexplicitly involved business interest,5 and provides
that the decision's effect is material if:

• The decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross revenues, or
the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than
$1,000,000.00, or five percent of the business's annual gross revenues and the increase or
decrease is at least $10,000.00. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).)

• The decision may cause the business to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate expenses by equal to or more than $250,000.00, or one percent of the business's
annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500.00. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(3).)

• The official knows or has reason to know that the business has a real property interest that is
explicitly involved in the decision under Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(1) through (6), or
there is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a substantial effect on the
property. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)

Regulation 18702.2(c) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's
reasonably foreseeable effect on an official's real property interest in a leasehold, and provides that
the decision's effect is material if the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the lease; (2)
increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) Change the official's actual or
legally allowable use of the property; or (4) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the property.

Rickert Ranch owns the Ranch Property and leases it to RASI, and RASI subleases the
Ranch Property to Prather Ranch for cattle grazing. Rickert Ranch receives $10,000 per year for
permitting hunting activities on the Ranch Property. The Supervisor states that the Project would
not have any impact on, and would not interfere with, the current uses of the Ranch Property, and
that the Project would not have any impact on her, her husband, or her dependent children's
personal finances. Furthermore, the facts presented indicate only that the Supervisor generally
supports a rural lifestyle and that the Project is approximately one mile from the Ranch Property.
You provide no indication decisions relating to the Project would have any financial effect on any
of the Supervisor's interests at issue. Therefore, based on the facts presented, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that decisions relating to the Project would have a material financial effect on the
Supervisor's interests in Rickert Ranch, RASI, or Prather Ranch; her various real property interests
in the Ranch Property; or her interest in her personal finances or those of immediate family. Thus,
the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in those decisions.6

5 Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that if the source of income is a business entity, materiality is determined
pursuant to Regulation 18702.1.

6 We caution that this advice is based on the facts provided and we have not been provided any indication of
the current use of the existing project site. To the extent that the current use of the property may affect the Supervisor's
various business interests, the conclusions reached in the letter do not apply. For example, if the project site is currently
used for agricultural purposes or commercial hunting operations potentially competing with the Supervisor's business
interests, the supervisor should seek additional advice.

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 6

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits a public officer or employee, while acting in his or her
official capacity, from making or participating in the making of a contract in which the officer or
employee is financially interested. Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

The California Supreme Court has stated that the purpose of Section 1090 is to make certain
that "every public officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal interest,
when dealing with contracts in an official capacity." (Thomson, supra, at p. 650.) Eliminating
temptation for public officers and employees, obtaining their undivided loyalty, and avoiding the
perception of impropriety are important public policy goals in California. (Id. at p. 648.)
Consequently, Section 1090 applies without regard to whether actual fraud or dishonesty is
involved in the contracting process, the contract is fair to the public agency, or the public agency
loses money from the contract. (Id. at pp. 648-649.)

Under Section 1090, a member of a public agency's governing body is conclusively
presumed to participate in the making of a contract under the governing body's authority,
irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of that contract. (Id. at pp. 649-
650.) Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the entire body from entering into a contract in which a
member of the body is financially interested, even if that member abstains from participating in the
making of, or fully discloses his or her financial interest in, the contract. (Ibid.)

The Supervisor is subject to Section 1090 because Section 1090 applies to county
supervisors. A contract is involved because a development agreement is a contract for purposes of
Section 1090. (Wilkins Advice Letter, No. A-18-227; 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 (1995).) The
Supervisor will be conclusively presumed to participate in the making of the potential development
agreement for the Project because that contract is within the Board of Supervisors' authority.
Therefore, if the Supervisor is financially interested in the potential development agreement for the
Project, Section 1090 would prohibit her from making or participating in making of, or the City
from entering into, that contract unless a statutory exception or the rule of necessity applies.

Accordingly, we now consider whether the Supervisor is financially interested in the
potential development agreement for the Project for purposes of Section 1090. Although Section
1090 does not specifically define "financial interest," an examination of case law and the statutory
exceptions to Section 1090's basic prohibition indicate that the term is to be liberally interpreted.
"However devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the forbidden
contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void." (People v. Deysher
(1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146.) Furthermore, "the certainty of financial gain is not necessary to create a
conflict of interest … The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public
officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as
where he [or she] acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298
(citations omitted).)

File No. A-19-179
Page No. 7

The Supervisor states that the Project would not have any impact on, and would not interfere with, any of the current uses of the Ranch Property, or have any impact on her, her husband, or her dependent children's personal finances. The Ranch Property is approximately one mile from the Project Site. Although the Project may increase the intensity of use of the immediate area surrounding the Project Site, and an increase in traffic on the roads accessing that area, there is sufficient distance between the Project Site and the Ranch Property to substantially mitigate those Project impacts. Therefore, we find that the Supervisor is not financially interested in decisions relating to a development agreement for the Project for purposes of Section 1090. Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, that contract.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By:    Brian G. Lau
       Assistant General Counsel

BGL:aja

parcels, given that the Supervisor has various financial interests a~sociated with a 5,000-acre real 
property located approximately one mile from the project site? 
(2) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Supervisor from participating in the making of, or the 
Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the project?

(1) No. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Supervisor from taking 
part in decisions relating to the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions 
would have a material financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests at issue based 
on the facts presented. 
(2) No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in the 
making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the 
project.

Section 1090 generally prohibits a public officer or employee, while acting in his or her 
official capacity, from making or participating in the making of a contract in which the officer or 
employee is financially interested. Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual 
impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey 
(1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call 
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
The California Supreme Court has stated that the purpose of Section 1090 is to make certain 
that "every public officer be guided solely by the public interest, rather than by personal interest, 
when dealing with contracts in an official capacity." (Thomson, supra, at p. 650.) Eliminating 
temptation for public officers and employees, obtaining their undivided loyalty, and avoiding the 
perception of impropriety are important public policy goals in California. (Id. at p. 648.) 
Consequently, Section 1090 applies without regard to whether actual fraud or dishpnesty is 
involved in the contracting process, the contract is fair to the public agency, or the public agency 
loses money from the contract. (Id. at pp. 648-649.) 
Und.er Section 1090, a member of a public agency's governing body is conclusively 
presumed to participate in the making of a contract under the governing body's authority, 
irrespective of whether he or she actually participates in the making of that contract. (Id. at pp. 649-
650.) Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the entire body from entering into a contract in which a 
member of the body is financially interested, even if that member abstains from participating in the 
making of, or fully discloses his or her financial interest in, the contract. (Ibid.) 
The Supervisor is subject to Section 1090 because Section 1090 applies to county 
supervisors. A contract is involved because a development agreement is a contract for purposes of 
Section 1090. (Wilkins Advice Letter, No. A-18-227; 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 (1995).) The 
Supervisor will be conclusively presumed to participate in the making of the potential development 
agreement for the Project because that contract is within the Board of Supervisors' authority. 
Therefore, if the Supervisor is financially interested in the potential development agreement for the 
Project, Section 1090 would prohibit her from making or participating in making of, or the City 
from entering into, that contract unless a statutory exception or the rule of necessity applies. 
Accordingly, we now consider whether the Supervisor is financially interested in the 
potential development agreement for the Project for purposes of Section 1090. Although Section 
1090 does not specifically define "financial interest," an examination of case law and the statutory 
exceptions to Section 1090's basic prohibition indicate that the term is to be liberally interpreted. 
"However devious and winding the chain may be which connects the officer with the forbidden 
contract, if it can be followed and the connection made, the contract is void." (People v. Deysher 
(1934) 2 Cal.2d 141, 146.) Furthermore, "the certainty. of financial gain is not necessary to create a 
conflict of interest ... The government's right to the absolute, undivided allegiance of a public 
officer is diminished as effectively where the officer acts with a hope of personal financial gain as 
where he [or she] acts with certainty." (People v. Gnass (2002) 101 Cal.App.4th 1271, 1298 
(citations omitted).)  
The Supervisor states that the Project would not have any impact on, and would not interfere 
with, any of the current uses of the Ranch Property, or have any impact on her, her husband, or her 
dependent children's personal finances. The Ranch Property is approximately one mile from the 
Project Site. Although the Project may increase the intensity of use of the immediate area 
surrounding the Project Site, and an increase in traffic on the roads accessing that area, there is 
sufficient distance between the Project Site and the Ranch Property to substantially mitigate those 
Project impacts. Therefore, we find that the Supervisor is not financially interested in decisions 
relating to a development agreement for the Project for purposes of Section 1090. Accordingly, 
Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in making of, or the 
Board of Supervisors from entering into, that contract. 
·

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: ( 1) Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Supervisor Rickert from taking part 
in governmental decisions relating to a proposed residential development project, which would 
divide the approximately 715-acre project site into 166 residential parcels and six large open space 
parcels, given that the Supervisor has various financial interests a~sociated with a 5,000-acre real 
property located approximately one mile from the project site? 
(2) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Supervisor from participating in the making of, or the 
Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the project?

CONCLUSION: (1) No. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Supervisor from taking 
part in decisions relating to the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions 
would have a material financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests at issue based 
on the facts presented. 
(2) No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in the 
making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the 
project.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 January 7, 2020 Rubin E. Cruse, Jr. County Counsel County of Shasta 1450 Court Street, Suite 332 Redding, CA 96001-1675 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-179 Dear Mr. Cruse: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of County of Shasta Supervisor Mary Rickert regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the Act's conflict of interest provisions and Section 1090 and not under any other conflict of interest laws. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts relating to it to the Attorney General's Office and the Shasta County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS (1) Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Supervisor Rickert from taking part in governmental decisions relating to a proposed residential development project, which would divide the approximately 715-acre project site into 166 residential parcels and six large open space parcels, given that the Supervisor has various financial interests associated with a 5,000-acre real property located approximately one mile from the project site? (2) Does Section 1090 prohibit the Supervisor from participating in the making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the project? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-179 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS (1) No. The Act's conflict of interest provisions do not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in decisions relating to the project because it is not reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on any of the Supervisor's financial interests at issue based on the facts presented. (2) No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in the making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, a development agreement approving the project. FACTS AS PRESENTED
